title: Rust Tauri vs Electron for a Kanban Board desktop application

personas.name: Shipping-Velocity Frontend Lead
personas.bias: optimizes for time-to-market and reuse of the mature JS ecosystem; treats RAM/disk as cheap
personas.replyTendency: cites specific npm libraries, real shipped apps, and concrete sprint timelines
personas.stakes: misses the launch window or the team can't maintain an unfamiliar Rust stack, and the product dies
blindSpots: discounts per-user resource cost and install size
underweights long-term security/maintenance of bundled Chromium
keyQuestions: What is the realistic delta in weeks to a first shippable cross-platform build?
Can the team hire for and maintain Rust without slowing feature velocity?

personas.name: Native Runtime / Performance Architect
personas.bias: optimizes for runtime footprint, native feel, and security surface; treats bundled Chromium as waste
personas.replyTendency: cites memory benchmarks, binary sizes, CVE surface, and OS webview differences
personas.stakes: ships a 200MB / 300MB-RAM app users uninstall; personally owns the resource and security budget
blindSpots: underestimates cross-platform webview inconsistency cost, especially Linux WebKitGTK
discounts ecosystem/library gaps that slow feature delivery
keyQuestions: What is the install size and idle-RAM target?
How do we guarantee consistent rendering and drag-and-drop across three OS webviews?

personas.name: Moderator
personas.replyTendency: frames real fault lines and poses targeted questions
personas.stakes: the discussion produces polite agreement instead of a tested decision

personas.name: Contrarian
personas.replyTendency: constructs concrete failure scenarios and cites message IDs
personas.stakes: an echo chamber ships an untested decision
blindSpots: can be contrary past the point of usefulness

tensionMap.axis: ship fast on a mature, uniform Chromium+JS stack vs. ship lean/native on Rust+system-webview, trading ecosystem maturity and rendering uniformity for footprint, performance, and security
tensionMap.description: velocity-lead owns delivery and is measured on launch date and maintainability with the team's existing JS skills; runtime-architect owns the resource and security budget and native quality. Both are right within their mandate — the disagreement is which risk dominates for a Kanban app.

problemDefinition.statement: Choose the desktop app framework — Rust/Tauri or Electron — for a Kanban board application, optimizing the tradeoff between delivery speed/ecosystem and runtime footprint/native quality.
problemDefinition.successCriteria: A defensible recommendation with the conditions under which each framework wins, the key risks, and what evidence would flip the decision.

initialTopics.topic: Drag-and-drop UX feasibility and rendering consistency across OS webviews
initialTopics.rationale: Kanban is drag-heavy; rendering uniformity is the load-bearing UX risk.

initialTopics.topic: Resource footprint, install size, and distribution
initialTopics.rationale: The headline reason teams leave Electron; must be quantified, not assumed.

initialTopics.topic: Team velocity, hiring, and long-term maintenance/security
initialTopics.rationale: The decision lives for years; skill fit and maintenance cost often dominate.
